title: L06 · Nominative & Accusative
objective: German has four 'cases' that mark a noun's role in the sentence. This lesson covers the two you'll meet first: the Nominative (subject) and the Accusative (direct object). Master these and 60% of sentences will make sense.
strategy: The big idea: articles CHANGE shape depending on the role of the noun. Only the masculine article actually changes between nom. and acc. (der → den, ein → einen). Feminine, neuter, and plural stay the same. So the practical thing to learn: 'der → den, ein → einen' for masculine direct objects.

sections.title: What is a 'case'?
sections.note: In English we figure out who-does-what by word order: 'The dog bites the man' vs 'The man bites the dog'. In German, ARTICLES (and pronouns) carry that information — so word order can be flexible.

blocks.text: Nominative = subject (who is doing). Accusative = direct object (who is being affected).
blocks.rule: Nominative answers 'who/what does the action'. Accusative answers 'whom/what is being affected'.

examples.translation: The man sees the dog. / El hombre ve al perro.
It's the dog that the man sees. (Object first for emphasis — meaning unchanged.) / Al perro lo ve el hombre.
examples.ipa: Even with reversed order, 'den' marks Hund as object and 'der' marks Mann as subject.

sections.title: The article table — nominative vs accusative
sections.note: ONLY MASCULINE CHANGES. Fem/neut/plur articles look identical in both cases.

blocks.text: Definite article: der → DEN (masc. only), die/das/die unchanged
blocks.audioText: der Mann → den Mann, die Frau → die Frau, das Kind → das Kind, die Leute → die Leute
blocks.rule: If you remember ONE thing: 'der' becomes 'den' when it's a direct object. Everything else stays the same.

examples.text: Nom: der Mann | Acc: den Mann
examples.translation: the man — masc. (changes) / el hombre

examples.text: Nom: die Frau | Acc: die Frau
examples.translation: the woman — fem. (no change) / la mujer

examples.text: Nom: das Kind | Acc: das Kind
examples.translation: the child — neut. (no change) / el niño

examples.text: Nom: die Häuser | Acc: die Häuser
examples.translation: the houses — plur. (no change) / las casas

blocks.text: Indefinite article: ein → EINEN (masc.), eine/ein/— unchanged
blocks.audioText: ein Mann → einen Mann, eine Frau → eine Frau, ein Kind → ein Kind
blocks.rule: Same idea: 'ein' becomes 'einen' for a masculine direct object. Fem 'eine', neut 'ein' stay put. Plural has no indefinite article (just drop it).

examples.text: Nom: ein Mann | Acc: einen Mann
examples.translation: a man (changes)

examples.text: Nom: eine Frau | Acc: eine Frau
examples.translation: a woman (no change)

examples.text: Nom: ein Kind | Acc: ein Kind
examples.translation: a child (no change)

blocks.text: Possessives (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr/Ihr) follow the 'ein' pattern
blocks.audioText: mein Vater → meinen Vater, meine Mutter → meine Mutter
blocks.rule: Possessive adjectives use the SAME endings as 'ein/eine'. So 'mein' → 'meinen' for masculine direct objects.

examples.translation: I see my father. / Veo a mi padre.
I see my mother. (no change for fem.)
I see my child.
I see my children.

sections.title: Pronouns also change
sections.note: When 'me/you/him/her' is the object, the pronoun changes form. Memorise these — they're high-frequency.

blocks.text: Object pronouns: mich, dich, ihn / sie / es, uns, euch, sie / Sie
blocks.audioText: Ich sehe dich. Du siehst mich. Er sieht sie.
blocks.rule: Subject form → object form: ich→mich, du→dich, er→ihn, sie→sie, es→es, wir→uns, ihr→euch, sie/Sie→sie/Sie. (Note: 'sie' and 'es' and the plural 'sie/Sie' don't change.)

examples.translation: I see you. / Te veo.
You see me. / Me ves.
He sees him. / Lo ve.
She sees us. / Nos ve.
We see you all. / Os vemos.

sections.title: Verbs that always take accusative
sections.note: Most verbs that have a direct object trigger accusative. These are the most common.

blocks.audioText: Ich habe einen Hund. Ich habe eine Katze. Ich habe ein Auto.
blocks.rule: Whatever you HAVE is in the accusative.

examples.translation: I have a dog. / Tengo un perro.
I have a cat. / Tengo un gato.
I have a car. / Tengo un coche.
Do you have a brother? / ¿Tienes un hermano?

blocks.audioText: Ich sehe den Mann. Ich sehe die Frau. Ich sehe das Kind.

examples.translation: I see the man. / Veo al hombre.
We are watching the film. / Vemos la película.
I'm buying an apple. / Compro una manzana.
She buys a book. / Ella compra un libro.

blocks.rule: Stem-changes in du/er: du isst, er isst. Note 'isst' (one s) because the stem 'ess-' already ends in -ss and the ending -t replaces the final s.

examples.translation: I'm eating a bread roll. / Como un panecillo.
He's eating an apple. / Él come una manzana.
I'm drinking a coffee. / Tomo un café.
Are you drinking water? / ¿Bebes agua?

blocks.translation: to look for, to search / buscar

examples.translation: I'm looking for my key. / Busco mi llave.
We're looking for a hotel. / Buscamos un hotel.

sections.title: 'es gibt' — 'there is / there are'
sections.note: An idiom that ALWAYS takes accusative. Used constantly.

blocks.text: es gibt + ACCUSATIVE = there is / there are
blocks.audioText: Es gibt einen Park. Es gibt eine Bäckerei. Es gibt ein Problem.
blocks.rule: Literally 'it gives' — but means 'there exists'. Whatever exists is in the accusative.

examples.translation: There is a park. / Hay un parque.

examples.text: Es gibt eine Bäckerei in der Nähe.
examples.audioText: Es gibt eine Bäckerei in der Nähe.
examples.translation: There is a bakery nearby. / Hay una panadería cerca.
There is a problem. / Hay un problema.

examples.text: Es gibt viele Studenten in Berlin.
examples.audioText: Es gibt viele Studenten in Berlin.
examples.translation: There are many students in Berlin. / Hay muchos estudiantes en Berlín.
